critic_raw: The main concern is the presence of heavy augmentation with oversampling of rare positive examples in a dataset with only 410 true defects. When the training pipeline creates many near-duplicate images of positive examples, there is a risk that augmented versions of training positives end up in validation or test folds, inflating performance through data leakage. In a nested cross-validation setup with 410 positives, even a single original positive image that is augmented across folds could produce multiple near-identical evaluation examples. There is also a question about whether the 'part-number clustering' check was sufficient: if the same physical part was imaged multiple times across the production week and not all near-duplicate shots were caught by the grouping check, test performance could still be artificially inflated. The final test set from a 'later production week' could still include near-duplicates of training parts if part-level grouping was imperfect.
defender_raw: The methodology is sound because the critical sequence is: split first, then augment. All augmentations are generated on the fly inside the training fold only. The validation and test data contain only raw, unaugmented images. The nested cross-validation performs splits at the original-image level before any augmentation occurs. Part-number clustering prevents near-identical shots of the same physical unit from crossing split boundaries. The final test set consists of raw images from a later production week — a temporal holdout that provides additional protection against near-duplicate contamination. The claim is explicitly limited to this camera setup and manufacturing process, so cross-factory generalization is not being asserted.
adjudication_raw: The critic raises a legitimate structural concern about augmentation-induced leakage, but the defender identifies that the methodology explicitly prevents this by (1) splitting before augmenting, (2) generating augmentations on-the-fly within training folds only, and (3) using part-level clustering to prevent near-duplicate crossings. The later-week test set provides temporal separation. The critic's concern about imperfect part-level grouping is a valid verification point but does not establish that leakage occurred — it establishes that the audit should be verified. Given the prompt describes the checks as having been performed, defense wins on the stated methodology.
all_issues_raised: possibility that later-week test set includes near-duplicates of training parts
